title: Vignette Workflow
text:
Vignettes are narrative, example-driven reference documents — a term borrowed from R packages, where vignettes serve as long-form guides that complement terse function documentation. In this repo, vignettes serve the same role: they document decisions, workflows, guidelines, and techniques that are too contextual or discursive to live in a code comment or README.
A vignette is the right format when:

A workflow involves multiple steps and decisions that benefit from explanation
A guideline needs rationale, not just a rule
A technique is used infrequently enough that you’ll need a refresher next time
A decision was made deliberately and you want to document why

Vignettes are not conversation logs, analysis scripts, or prompt files. Those live in conversations/ and prompts/ respectively.

title: Vignette Workflow
text:
Why Quarto
Vignettes in this repo are written as Quarto documents (.qmd). Quarto was chosen because:

It supports both R and Python code chunks in the same document
It renders cleanly to HTML for browser-based reading
It integrates with Positron’s Viewer pane for local preview
It is not tied to the R package ecosystem, making it appropriate for a general-purpose mixed-language repo

title: Vignette Workflow
text:
Why a Website (Not Just Files)
Markdown files can be read directly in any editor, but rendered HTML is easier to navigate, especially for documents with tables, callout blocks, code examples, and a table of contents. The Quarto website setup adds:

A listing page that auto-discovers vignettes — no manual index to maintain
A navbar for quick navigation
Consistent formatting across all vignettes

title: Vignette Workflow
text:
Why GitHub Pages
The rendered site is published automatically to GitHub Pages via a GitHub Actions workflow. Every push to main triggers a rebuild. This means the published site is always in sync with the repo without any manual deployment steps.
The live site is available at: https://brad-cannell.github.io/my-ai-assistant/

title: Vignette Workflow
text:
1. Copy the template
Start from _vignette-template.qmd in the vignettes/ folder. The leading underscore prevents it from being rendered as part of the site.
cp vignettes/_vignette-template.qmd vignettes/your-vignette-name.qmd

title: Vignette Workflow
text:
2. Follow naming conventions
Use kebab-case, all lowercase, no spaces:



Pattern
Example




✅ Good
api-vs-browser.qmd


❌ Avoid
API vs Browser.qmd, api_vs_browser.qmd



The filename becomes the URL, so spaces and mixed case create problems.

title: Vignette Workflow
text:
3. Fill in the YAML header
At minimum, update these fields before writing any content:
---
title: "Your Vignette Title"
date: "YYYY-MM-DD"
date-modified: "YYYY-MM-DD"
version: "1.0"
description: "One sentence describing what this vignette covers."
categories: [relevant, categories, here]
---
See existing vignettes for examples. The description and categories fields appear in the listing page on the site — keep them concise and accurate.

title: Vignette Workflow
text:
4. Write the content
See the comments inside _vignette-template.qmd for guidance on content structure, heading levels, callout types, and cross-references. The short version:

Start with a # Overview or purpose statement
Use # for top-level sections, ## for subsections
Use prose for conceptual content, numbered steps for procedures
Use ```bash for terminal commands, ```{python} or ```{r} for executable code chunks
Use :::callout-note, :::callout-important, or :::callout-warning for asides and caveats

title: Vignette Workflow
text:
Updating content
When you revise a vignette:

Update the content
Bump date-modified to today’s date
Increment version — minor version (1.0 → 1.1) for small refinements, major version (1.1 → 2.0) for structural changes or revised recommendations

See vignettes/git-tags.qmd for the full version numbering convention and the Git tagging workflow for marking significant versions.

title: Vignette Workflow
section: When to create a new vignette vs. update an existing one
text:
When to create a new vignette vs. update an existing one
Update an existing vignette when the new content is a natural extension of what’s already there. Create a new vignette when the topic is distinct enough to stand alone and be discovered independently.

title: Vignette Workflow
text:
Locally (Positron Viewer pane or browser)
From the repo root:
quarto preview index.qmd
This renders all vignettes, starts a local dev server, and opens the site in Positron’s Viewer pane or your default browser. The preview hot-reloads on save — edits to any vignette are reflected immediately without restarting.

title: Vignette Workflow
text:
Online
The published site is rebuilt automatically on every push to main and is available at:
https://brad-cannell.github.io/my-ai-assistant/
No manual deployment steps are needed. After pushing changes, allow 2–3 minutes for the GitHub Actions workflow to complete before the live site reflects your updates. Build status can be monitored in the Actions tab of the GitHub repository.

title: UV Virtual Environments
text:
This vignette summarizes a clean, reproducible workflow for managing Python dependencies with uv, especially when working across multiple computers with a synced project folder (e.g., Box, Dropbox, OneDrive).
For the standard Python venv approach, see vignettes/venv-virtual-environments.qmd.

title: UV Virtual Environments
section: 1. Initialize uv in the project root
text:
1. Initialize uv in the project root
uv init
Creates (or updates):

pyproject.toml — dependency intent
No virtual environment yet

Commit pyproject.toml.

title: UV Virtual Environments
text:
2. Add dependencies
uv add pandas numpy matplotlib
This will:

Add dependencies to pyproject.toml
Generate uv.lock with exact versions

Commit both pyproject.toml and uv.lock.

title: UV Virtual Environments
text:
3. Create a local virtual environment
uv venv
Creates a .venv/ directory. This directory is machine-local and should not be synced.

title: UV Virtual Environments
text:
4. Install dependencies from the lockfile
uv sync
This installs exactly the versions recorded in uv.lock.
On a second machine:
uv venv
uv sync

title: Prompt Format
text:
In this project, prompts are treated as long-lived knowledge artifacts, not disposable strings. Writing prompts as Markdown documents with YAML headers supports this goal by balancing clarity, durability, and future extensibility with minimal overhead.



Encourages clear, well-structured prompts that separate roles, context, tasks, and constraints
Improves human readability, review, and collaboration
Integrates naturally with Git-based workflows, enabling meaningful diffs and version history
Supports disciplined iteration over time, especially in academic and pedagogical settings
Remains lightweight and tool-agnostic, avoiding lock-in to any specific platform or API





Capture stable, high-level metadata about each prompt (purpose, audience, domain, status)
Improve organization and discoverability without requiring tooling or automation
Enable future capabilities such as filtering, indexing, logging, and semantic search
Keep execution details and API-specific parameters out of the prompt definition
Are human-readable first, remaining useful even if no parsing system is ever built





Prompts are treated as maintainable assets, not transient inputs
Structure is used to support thinking, not constrain it
Metadata is included only where it provides clear, lasting value
The format should scale gracefully from manual use today to API-driven workflows tomorrow

Together, Markdown and YAML provide a simple, durable foundation for managing prompts as part of a larger, reproducible knowledge system while preserving flexibility as tools, models, and workflows evolve.

Prompts are treated as maintainable assets, not transient inputs

title: API vs Browser-Based AI Workflows
text: This document provides decision criteria and operational guidelines for choosing between browser-based AI interaction (Claude.ai) and direct API calls (via Chatlas in Quarto) for AI-assisted tasks. The goal is to minimize friction, maximize reproducibility where it matters, and avoid making the mode decision ad hoc.

title: API vs Browser-Based AI Workflows
text:
Use the Browser When:

The task is genuinely exploratory (“let me think out loud with Claude”)
You expect to use the output once and not reference it again
You are away from your development environment (no access to repo/terminal)
You need to upload a file for quick extraction or summarization and have no pipeline for it
The task is low-stakes and iteration speed matters more than documentation
You are troubleshooting or debugging interactively and expect many rapid back-and-forth exchanges

title: API vs Browser-Based AI Workflows
text:
Use the API When:

You have used a similar prompt before, or expect to use it again
The output will be incorporated into a document, codebase, or artifact you are maintaining (EWB lesson, grant section, manuscript, R script)
Reproducibility matters — you may need to document what was sent, what parameters were used, and what was returned
The task involves file context that is structured and reusable (formatting guidelines, CST examples, style rules)
You are building or maintaining a pipeline (EWB lesson creation, batch content review)
You want parameter control (model selection, temperature, system prompt)
The task involves sensitive research content where better data handling terms apply
You are teaching or demonstrating AI usage and want a documentable, transferable example
The task would benefit from a model other than the browser default — e.g., a larger context window for full document analysis, or a faster/cheaper model for batch or repetitive work
The task is high-volume or cost-sensitive and strategic model selection would meaningfully reduce cost or processing time

title: API vs Browser-Based AI Workflows
text:
Boundary Cases







Situation
Recommendation




Quick code fix, one function
Browser — unless it’s part of a documented pipeline


Drafting an email from a template
API — prompt is reusable; output may be archived


Uploading a PDF for a one-time summary
Browser


Uploading a file as structured context for ongoing work
API


Brainstorming grant aims
Browser — then migrate key outputs to API doc or notes


Refining specific aims language
API


Asking “does this paragraph make sense?”
Browser


Full manuscript section review
API

title: Git Tags
text: Scope: Strategies for tracking version history on individual files within a Git repo, and retrieving prior versions when needed. Oriented toward documents and scripts in an academic workflow (vignettes, SOPs, prompts, analysis files).

title: Git Tags
text:
1. Background
Git tracks changes at the commit level, not the file level. Every commit is a snapshot of the entire repo at a point in time. This means there is no native Git concept of “version 2.0 of this specific file” — but you can approximate it reliably using a combination of:

Version numbers embedded in the file itself (YAML front matter)
Git tags scoped by naming convention to a specific file
Git log filtered to a specific file to retrieve the full commit history for that file

Used together, these three tools give you a practical, lightweight versioning system for individual documents without requiring any additional infrastructure.

title: Git Tags
text:
2. Embedding Version Numbers in Files
The simplest and most portable approach is to store the version number in the file’s own YAML front matter. This makes the version visible whenever you open the file, independent of any Git tooling.
---
title: "My Document"
date: "2026-03-17"
date-modified: "2026-03-17"
version: "2.0"
---
Conventions for updating the version field are covered in Section 5. The key discipline is keeping the version in the file synchronized with the Git tag you create at the same time (see Section 3).

title: Git Tags
text:
3. Tagging a Specific File Version
After committing a meaningful update to a file, create a Git tag named to identify both the file and the version. Tags point to commits, not files — but a consistent naming convention makes them behave like file-level version markers.

Workflow
# 1. Make your edits to the file, then stage and commit
git add vignettes/api-vs-browser.qmd
git commit -m "Revise api-vs-browser to v2.0"

# 2. Create a tag referencing the file and version
git tag sop-api-vs-browser-v2.0

# 3. Push the commit and the tag to the remote
git push origin main
git push origin sop-api-vs-browser-v2.0


Tag Naming Convention
Use the pattern: {file-identifier}-v{version}



File
Tag




vignettes/api-vs-browser.qmd
sop-api-vs-browser-v2.0


vignettes/git-tags.qmd
git-tags-v1.0


prompts/ewb-content-review.md
prompt-ewb-content-review-v1.2


R/clean-detect.R
script-clean-detect-v3.1



The file identifier does not need to match the filename exactly — it just needs to be unambiguous and consistent. Hyphens are preferred over underscores in tag names (Git allows both, but hyphens are conventional).


Listing All Tags for a File
git tag --list "sop-api-vs-browser-*"

title: Git Tags
text:
4. Retrieving a Previous Version
There are two retrieval scenarios: viewing a prior version, and restoring it.

4a. View a Prior Version Without Changing Anything
Use a tag to view the file as it existed at that version. This does not affect your working directory.
# View via tag
git show sop-api-vs-browser-v1.0:"vignettes/api-vs-browser.qmd"

# Redirect output to a file for closer review
git show sop-api-vs-browser-v1.0:"vignettes/api-vs-browser.qmd" > /tmp/api-vs-browser-v1.qmd


4b. Find a Prior Version Without a Tag
If you did not tag a specific version, use git log filtered to the file to find the relevant commit hash.
# List all commits that touched a specific file
git log --oneline -- vignettes/api-vs-browser.qmd
Output will look like:
a3f92c1 Revise api-vs-browser to v2.0
7d84be0 Add security considerations section
2c10fa4 Initial commit of api-vs-browser vignette
Use the commit hash to view or restore the file:
# View the file at a specific commit
git show 7d84be0:"vignettes/api-vs-browser.qmd"


4c. Restore a Prior Version to Your Working Directory
If you want to roll back the file to a prior version:
# Restore the file from a tag
git checkout sop-api-vs-browser-v1.0 -- vignettes/api-vs-browser.qmd

# Or restore from a commit hash
git checkout 7d84be0 -- vignettes/api-vs-browser.qmd
This overwrites the current version of the file in your working directory. The change is staged but not committed — review it first, then commit if you want to keep it.
git diff --staged vignettes/api-vs-browser.qmd      # review the change
git commit -m "Restore api-vs-browser to v1.0"      # commit if correct
git restore --staged vignettes/api-vs-browser.qmd   # or discard if not

title: Git Tags
text:
5. Version Number Conventions
Use a two-part version number: {major}.{minor}, following these guidelines.

When to Increment the Minor Version (x.1, x.2 …)
Increment the minor version for changes that refine or extend the document without changing its fundamental structure or purpose.

Adding a new boundary case row to a decision table
Correcting a factual error or updating an outdated reference
Clarifying language without changing meaning
Adding a new bullet to an existing list
Small structural improvements (reordering sections, improving headings)

Examples: 1.0 → 1.1, 2.3 → 2.4


When to Increment the Major Version (1.x → 2.0)
Increment the major version for changes that substantially alter the document’s scope, structure, or conclusions — changes where a reader familiar with the prior version would notice a meaningful difference.

Adding or removing entire sections
Changing the core recommendation or decision rule
Merging content from another document
Revising the document’s stated scope or purpose
Any change significant enough that prior references to the document may no longer be accurate

Examples: 1.1 → 2.0, 2.4 → 3.0


Starting Version
New documents start at 1.0, not 0.1 or 0.9. A document committed to the repo is a document — there is no meaningful distinction between a “draft” and “version 1.0” for personal workflow documents. If it is not ready to commit, do not commit it.


Version Numbers for Different File Types







File Type
Version Tracking Approach




SOPs and vignettes (.qmd)
YAML front matter + Git tag


Prompt files (.md)
YAML front matter + Git tag


R/Python scripts
Comment header + Git tag for major milestones only


Data files
Git tag only (no front matter); consider a _provenance.md companion file


Quarto conversation documents
No versioning needed — these are logs, not living documents





What Not to Version
Not every file needs explicit version numbers. Reserve the tagging workflow for files that are:

Actively revised over time
Referenced by name from other documents or SOPs
Stable enough that prior versions have archival value

One-off analysis scripts, scratch files, and conversation logs do not benefit from explicit versioning — Git’s commit history is sufficient for those.

title: Git Tags
text:
6. Quick Reference

Tag a new version after committing
git tag {file-identifier}-v{version}
git push origin {file-identifier}-v{version}


List all tags for a file
git tag --list "{file-identifier}-*"


View a prior version (no changes to working directory)
git show {tag}:"path/to/file.qmd"


Find prior versions without a tag
git log --oneline -- path/to/file.qmd


Restore a prior version to working directory
git checkout {tag or hash} -- path/to/file.qmd

title: Working with Python and Chatlas
text: This vignette provides information about using Python and Chatlas to interact with AI assistants. It describes the conceptual role of each step in a typical API conversation workflow, and documents less-common techniques not covered in the conversation templates. For working code for routine tasks, see the template conversation files in conversations/.

title: Working with Python and Chatlas
text:
Load Packages
Every conversation document begins by loading the Python packages it depends on. This typically includes packages for interacting with the API (e.g., openai, chatlas), managing credentials (e.g., keyring), handling file paths and dates, and any local helper packages. Keeping all imports at the top of the document makes dependencies explicit and makes it easy to spot when a package needs to be installed.

title: Working with Python and Chatlas
text:
File Paths and Environment Variables
Declaring file paths, environment variables, and the model name near the top of the conversation document — rather than inline throughout the code — makes the document easier to reuse and adapt. When you need to point the conversation at a different file or switch models, there is a single place to make that change. This section typically sets today’s date, paths to any prompt templates or input files, and the model identifier.

title: Working with Python and Chatlas
text:
Load Helper Functions
Many conversations rely on local helper functions stored in a shared Python package within the repo. Loading these functions near the top of the document, after packages and file paths, keeps the rest of the conversation clean and focused on the task rather than implementation details.

title: Working with Python and Chatlas
text:
Expose API
API keys should never be hardcoded in a script or committed to Git. The standard approach in this repo is to store keys in the system keyring and retrieve them at runtime, then set them as environment variables for the duration of the session. This section handles that retrieval and confirms the key was found before proceeding.

title: Working with Python and Chatlas
text:
Read the Template Prompt
When using a prompt template stored in prompts/, this step reads the template file, extracts the system prompt and user message sections separately, and injects any dynamic values (e.g., lesson name, course name) using placeholder substitution. Keeping the prompt definition in a separate file — rather than inline in the conversation — makes prompts easier to version, review, and reuse.

title: Working with Python and Chatlas
section: Pass the Template Prompt to the AI Assistant
text:
Pass the Template Prompt to the AI Assistant
This step submits the system prompt and initial user message to the API to open the conversation. The model’s response to this first message is typically a confirmation that it has received and understood the instructions rather than substantive content — it establishes the context for the rest of the conversation. See OpenAI’s prompt engineering guide for background on message roles.

title: Working with Python and Chatlas
section: Pass Text File Contents to the AI Assistant
text:
Pass Text File Contents to the AI Assistant
After the conversation is initialized with the template prompt, this step passes the actual content to be worked on — typically a lesson file, draft, or document — as the next user message. The file is read as plain text and submitted directly. This is the most reliable approach for Quarto and Markdown files, which are not supported as native file uploads by the API (see Pass a File to the API below for alternatives when plain text is insufficient).

title: Working with Python and Chatlas
section: Write Output to a File for Review
text:
Write Output to a File for Review
After receiving a response, this step writes the model’s output to a Markdown file in a designated review folder. Saving the output as a file — rather than just printing it — makes it easier to review, diff against the original, and incorporate into the project. The filename typically includes today’s date to make outputs easy to sort and identify later.

title: Working with Python and Chatlas
text:
Follow-up and Refinement
Most conversations benefit from at least one follow-up exchange after the initial response. This step is intentionally open-ended: you might ask for revisions, request a different format, probe an unclear point, or ask for a summary. Two useful patterns are creating a fork of the chat object before sending a speculative follow-up (so you can discard it without losing the main thread), and using the interactive chat interface (chat.app()) for rapid back-and-forth during refinement.

title: Working with Python and Chatlas
text:
Save Chat History
Saving the full conversation history at the end of a session makes it possible to return to the conversation later, audit what was sent and received, or pass the history back to the model in a future session for continuity. The conversation templates export history in Markdown format for readability and in JSON format for programmatic use. Optionally, you can condense a long history by asking the model to summarize it before saving, which reduces the token cost of reloading it in a future session.

title: Working with Python and Chatlas
text:
Render to HTML
Rendering the conversation document to HTML produces a readable, shareable record of the full session — prompts, code, and outputs together. This is useful for documentation, review, and reproducibility. Render via the Preview button in Positron or by running quarto render path/to/conversation.qmd in the terminal.

title: Working with Python and Chatlas
Working with Python and Chatlas
section: When to Use a Vector Store
text:
When to Use a Vector Store

You have a document (or set of documents) you’ll query repeatedly
You want to ask questions grounded in specific file content
You need deterministic, source-traceable retrieval
The document is too large to pass as plain text in each call

title: Working with Python and Chatlas
text:
Setup
Vector stores are created and managed through the OpenAI developer dashboard:

Open the dashboard and navigate to Storage.
Create a new vector store and give it a name.
Upload your files and wait for indexing to complete (status will show as “ready”).
Copy the vector store ID — you’ll use it in your code.

title: Working with Python and Chatlas
text:
Query a Vector Store

from chatlas import ChatOpenAI

chat = ChatOpenAI(model=model)

vs_id = "your-vector-store-id"

# Ask a question grounded in the uploaded documents
resp = chat.chat(
    "Using ONLY the uploaded documents, answer the following question: "
    "[your question here]. "
    "If the answer is not explicitly stated in the documents, say so.",
    kwargs={
        "tools": [{"type": "file_search", "vector_store_ids": [vs_id]}]
    }
)

print(str(resp))

title: Working with Python and Chatlas
Working with Python and Chatlas
section: Multi-turn Conversations with a Vector Store
text:
Multi-turn Conversations with a Vector Store
To keep file_search available across multiple turns, pass the same kwargs on each call — or set it once at the chat level:

# Option 1: pass kwargs on each turn
resp1 = chat.chat("First question", kwargs={"tools": [{"type": "file_search", "vector_store_ids": [vs_id]}]})
resp2 = chat.chat("Follow-up question", kwargs={"tools": [{"type": "file_search", "vector_store_ids": [vs_id]}]})

# Option 2: set tools at the chat level (applies to all subsequent turns)
chat.set_tools([{"type": "file_search", "vector_store_ids": [vs_id]}])
resp1 = chat.chat("First question")
resp2 = chat.chat("Follow-up question")







Note



Vector storage is not free. Currently, storage costs $0.10/GB per day, with the first GB free. See OpenAI pricing for current rates. Delete vector stores you no longer need from the dashboard.

title: Working with Python and Chatlas
section: Method 1: Add the Website to a Vector Store (Recommended)
text:
Method 1: Add the Website to a Vector Store (Recommended)
Save the page as a PDF, upload it to a vector store, and query it with file_search. This is the most reliable approach because the content is fixed at upload time.
Steps:

Open the page in a browser and save it as a PDF.
Upload the PDF to your vector store (see Vector Stores above).
Query using file_search.


from chatlas import ChatOpenAI

chat = ChatOpenAI(model=model)

vs_id = "your-vector-store-id"

resp = chat.chat(
    "Using ONLY the uploaded page content, answer the following question: "
    "[your question here]. "
    "Return your answer as a bulleted list.",
    kwargs={
        "tools": [{"type": "file_search", "vector_store_ids": [vs_id]}],
        "include": ["file_search_call.action.sources"],  # optional: verify sources
    }
)

print(str(resp))

Pros: Deterministic; reusable; indexed once, queried many times; source-traceable.
Cons: Requires a manual save-to-PDF step; page content is fixed at upload time and will not reflect updates to the live page.

title: Working with Python and Chatlas
text:
Method 2: Live Web Search
Use the web_search tool to have the model retrieve and read a live page at query time. Add domain filtering to reduce the chance the model retrieves content from a different site.

from chatlas import ChatOpenAI

chat = ChatOpenAI(model=model)

url = "https://example.com/page-you-want-to-use"

# Basic web search
resp = chat.chat(
    f"Use web search to open and read this specific page:\n{url}\n\n"
    "Question: [your question here]\n\n"
    "Answer with a bulleted list.",
    kwargs={
        "tools": [{"type": "web_search"}],
        "tool_choice": "auto",
    },
)

print(str(resp))

To make results more deterministic with domain filtering (recommended):

resp = chat.chat(
    f"Use web search and rely on this page for the answer:\n{url}\n\n"
    "[Your question here]",
    kwargs={
        "tools": [
            {
                "type": "web_search",
                "filters": {
                    "allowed_domains": ["example.com"]
                },
            }
        ],
        "tool_choice": "auto",
    },
)

print(str(resp))

To request sources so you can verify what the model actually read:

resp = chat.chat(
    f"Use web search to answer from this page:\n{url}\n\n"
    "[Your question here]",
    kwargs={
        "tools": [{"type": "web_search"}],
        "tool_choice": "auto",
        "include": ["web_search_call.action.sources"],
    },
)

print(str(resp))







Note



If the model answers without actually searching, make the instruction more explicit: “Use web search now. Do not answer until the page has been opened.” Keep the domain filter on.
Web search is not free. Currently, it costs up to $25 per 1,000 calls. See OpenAI pricing for current rates.


Pros: Fast; no preprocessing required; reads the live, current version of the page.
Cons: Not deterministic (page content may change between calls); not reusable across sessions; not guaranteed the model actually reads the target page; not ideal for reproducible research workflows.

title: Working with Python and Chatlas
text:
Basic Usage
Create the prompt in the OpenAI dashboard, then reference it by ID:

from openai import OpenAI

client = OpenAI()

response = client.responses.create(
    prompt={
        "id": "your-prompt-id",
        "version": "1",          # pin to a specific version
        "variables": {           # inject dynamic values
            "variable_name": "variable_value"
        }
    }
)

print(response.output_text)

title: Working with Python and Chatlas
Venv Virtual Environments
text: This vignette covers creating and managing Python virtual environments using the built-in venv tool in Positron. For a modern, cross-machine alternative with lockfile support, see vignettes/uv-virtual-environments.qmd.

title: Venv Virtual Environments
section: Why This Matters for a Chat / API Project
text:
Why This Matters for a Chat / API Project
If you install packages globally:
pip install openai pydantic rich
Those versions become shared by every Python script on your machine — easy to accidentally upgrade or downgrade, and hard to reproduce later. This becomes painful when one project needs pydantic<2 and another needs pydantic>=2. A venv prevents this class of problems entirely.

title: Venv Virtual Environments
text:
Method 1: Command Palette (Recommended)
This is the fastest way to have Positron create and automatically register the environment for your current workspace.

Open your project folder in Positron.
Open the Command Palette: Cmd+Shift+P (macOS) or Ctrl+Shift+P (Windows/Linux).
Type and select Python: Create Environment….
Select venv as the environment provider.
Choose the Python interpreter version you want to use.

Positron will create a .venv/ folder in your project root and automatically set it as the active interpreter for your workspace.

title: Venv Virtual Environments
text:
Method 2: Manual Terminal Setup
If you prefer the command line or need specific configurations:

Open the integrated terminal in Positron.
Create the venv:

macOS/Linux: python3 -m venv .venv
Windows: python -m venv .venv

Activate the venv:

macOS/Linux: source .venv/bin/activate
Windows: .venv\Scripts\activate

Connect to Positron: click the Python interpreter version in the top-right corner (or use Python: Select Interpreter in the Command Palette) and select the path to the new .venv.
